metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the `A supersedes B` edge is stored on sibling A's `deps` (not on B), so `delivered_now` must scan the epic's children to find any sibling A whose `deps` contains `{relation: 'supersedes', target_id: B}` — and update the 'Supersede mechanism' section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan states B is identified as superseded by checking B's own deps ('reads it via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps`'; 'child B is delivered-elsewhere iff a SIBLING A … has an `A supersedes B` edge'), but in rebar's event-sourced model the `supersedes` link is stored on the SOURCE ticket A (as a dep entry with `relation='supersedes'`, `target_id=B`), NOT on B. Reading B's `deps` list will not reveal that A supersedes B — the implementation must scan sibling A's deps (or the epic's children) to find the outbound `supersedes` edge pointing at B.
findings.location: What section; Supersede mechanism section: 'child B is delivered-elsewhere iff a SIBLING A (same `parent_id`) has an `A supersedes B` edge (equivalently B `superseded_by` A)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the mechanism description to: 'to detect that B is superseded, iterate the epic's direct children (from `rebar.show_ticket(epic_id)`'s children or `rebar.deps(epic_id)["children"]`), check each sibling A's compiled-state `deps` for an entry with `relation="supersedes"` and `target_id == B.ticket_id`, then verify A is a live vehicle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the 'What' section that detecting 'B superseded_by A' requires scanning *sibling* tickets' `deps` (not B's own deps) for `{relation: 'supersedes', target_id: B}`, since `supersedes` is a directional, non-reciprocal link stored only on the source (A); spell out the O(siblings) traversal pattern (e.g., `rebar.list_tickets(parent=B.parent_id)` then `rebar.show_ticket(sib)['deps']` for each sibling, or `rebar.deps(epic)`).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify WHOSE `deps` are scanned to find the `A supersedes B` edge. Since `supersedes` is a directional, non-reciprocal relation stored ONLY on the source ticket A (confirmed: `_commands/unlink.py` documents 'duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from are directional (no reciprocal link)'; `_processors.py` only appends to the source's `deps`), child B's own `deps` will never contain a `supersedes` entry pointing away from B. Discovering 'B is superseded by A' requires iterating all siblings (tickets with the same `parent_id`) and checking each sibling's `deps` for `{relation: 'supersedes', target_id: B}`. This O(siblings) traversal — which is the only mechanically correct path — is absent from the plan, leaving the implementer likely to read B's own deps and find nothing.
findings.location: ## What — 'read the `supersedes`/`duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`; verify shared parent so work that left the epic does not count as delivered'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace 'read the `supersedes`/`duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`' with: 'enumerate siblings via `rebar.list_tickets(parent=child.parent_id, repo_root=repo_root)`, then for each sibling S call `rebar.show_ticket(S)` and inspect `S["deps"]` for an entry `{relation: "supersedes", target_id: child.ticket_id}` — since `supersedes` is directional/non-reciprocal and lives only on the superseder (S), not on the superseded child (B).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete API call for discovering that child B is superseded by sibling A: either `find_inbound_relationships(B_id, tracker_dir)` (which surfaces inbound `supersedes` links) or iterating siblings' `rebar.show_ticket(sib)['deps']` for `relation == 'supersedes'` and `target_id == B_id` — the plan's current 'read from the child's deps' phrasing names the wrong direction.
findings.finding: The plan says to read 'B superseded_by A' (direction: B superseded_by A) via 'rebar.show/ticket_deps', but no 'superseded_by' relation exists in the codebase — the edge is stored as an OUTGOING 'supersedes' dep on sibling A's record, not on B's. Calling rebar.deps(B) returns B's outbound deps and will not expose this edge; discovering it requires either find_inbound_relationships(B, tracker_dir) or iterating each sibling's outbound deps.
findings.location: ## What — 'read the `supersedes`/`duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`; verify shared parent' and Acceptance Criteria (mechanism) — 'The supersede signal is the structured `supersedes` link relation read from the child's deps'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and the AC mechanism, replace 'read from the child's deps' with 'read from sibling A's outbound deps (relation == "supersedes", target_id == child_B) OR use find_inbound_relationships(child_B, tracker_dir) to get inbound supersedes links directly on B'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the correct read direction for detecting 'B superseded_by A': either (a) iterate sibling ticket deps via `rebar.deps(sibling)` for each sibling with a `supersedes`→B edge, or (b) call `rebar.find_inbound_relationships(B, tracker_dir)` and filter for `relation='supersedes'` inbound entries, then verify each source is a sibling (same `parent_id`).
findings.finding: The plan instructs reading supersedes/duplicates links 'via src/rebar/__init__.py' (rebar.show/ticket_deps) applied to child B, but the 'supersedes' relation is stored as an OUTBOUND edge on A (A supersedes B), not on B. B's own deps[] will not contain a 'supersedes' entry pointing at it. Detecting 'B superseded_by A' requires either iterating all siblings' deps for a supersedes->B edge, or calling find_inbound_relationships(B) to get inbound links. The plan never specifies this read direction, leaving the core supersede detection mechanism unimplementable as written.
findings.location: What section: 'read the supersedes/duplicates links via src/rebar/__init__.py'; Supersede mechanism section: 'child B is delivered-elsewhere iff a SIBLING A (same parent_id) has an A supersedes B edge'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'read the supersedes/duplicates links via rebar.show/ticket_deps' with: 'call find_inbound_relationships(B, tracker_dir) (available as rebar.find_inbound_relationships, re-exported from rebar.__init__) and filter inbound_links for relation="supersedes"; each from_id is a candidate A — verify shared parent_id via rebar.show_ticket(A).parent_id == epic_parent_id.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the plan's reference to `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps` with the actual APIs: `rebar.show_ticket(ticket_id)` (returns compiled state including `deps[]`) or `rebar.deps(ticket_id)` (returns `{deps: [...], children: [...], ...}`), both confirmed to exist in `src/rebar/__init__.py`.
findings.finding: The plan references reading supersede links via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps` from `src/rebar/__init__.py`, but no function named `ticket_deps` exists anywhere in the codebase — the actual API is `rebar.deps(ticket_id)` (which wraps `deps_state`). This is an unverifiable assertion about a named artifact.
findings.location: What section: 'read the `supersedes`/`duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`'; Supersede mechanism section: 'reads it via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section and 'Supersede mechanism' section, replace 'via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps`' with 'via `rebar.show_ticket(ticket_id)` (compiled state's `deps` list) or `rebar.deps(ticket_id)`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for `delivered_now` when dep-graph reads fail (e.g., `ticket_deps`/`rebar.show` raises): the predicate SHOULD catch read/parse errors, log them, and return False (conservative default = not delivered), preventing a single bad ticket from aborting the full epic review.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `delivered_now` behaves when the dep-graph read (`ticket_deps`/`rebar.show`) fails — e.g., ticket not found, malformed link graph, or I/O error — leaving it ambiguous whether the predicate propagates exceptions (potentially crashing the epic review) or safely defaults to False (not delivered).
findings.location: What section / 'read the supersedes/duplicates links via src/rebar/__init__.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `ticket_deps` / `rebar.show` call in a try/except that logs a warning (including the child ticket ID and exception) and returns False, so a missing or malformed ticket is treated as 'not delivered' rather than propagating an exception up to the caller.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a note in What) specifying delivered_now's error fallback: e.g., 'Any I/O or read failure during the supersede check returns False (fail-closed), consistent with compute_validity's own error-handling pattern; errors are logged but never propagate as exceptions from the predicate.'
findings.finding: No AC or prose specifies what delivered_now returns when the dependency-graph read or rebar.show call fails (ticket not found, I/O error, malformed link graph). The predicate is described as having no fallback for sub-step failures, leaving the error path undefined — an executor must invent a policy (fail-closed returning False, raise, or log-and-skip).
findings.location: What section: 'Recomputed each run'; AC section: no fallback AC; Scope section: no error-handling mention
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What: 'Any exception during the sibling lookup or rebar.show reads is caught; delivered_now returns False on any read error (fail-closed, consistent with compute_validity's policy). Errors are logged at WARNING level.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the supersede-link reading API reference to a single, specific callable (e.g., `from rebar import ticket_deps; ticket_deps(child_id)`) in both the 'What' and 'Mechanism' sections.
findings.finding: The API for reading supersede links is referenced inconsistently: the 'What' section says 'read the `supersedes`/`duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`' while the mechanism section says 'reads it via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps`' — an executor cannot determine which concrete call to make without inspection.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace both references with one precise call site, e.g.: 'call `ticket_deps(child.id, repo_root)` (exported from `src/rebar/__init__.py`) to obtain the deps graph, then filter for edges of type `supersedes` where child is the target.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps` API references in the 'Supersede mechanism' section with the correct library symbols: `rebar.show_ticket(child_id, repo_root=repo_root)` for ticket state (including `parent_id`) and `rebar.deps(child_id, repo_root=repo_root)` for the dep-graph (including `deps[].relation` and `deps[].target_id`).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Supersede mechanism' section names `rebar.show` and `ticket_deps` as the read APIs for fetching dep-graph links, but neither symbol exists in the library (`src/rebar/__init__.py`). The actual library functions are `rebar.show_ticket()` (line 629) for ticket state and `rebar.deps()` (line 817) for the dependency graph. `ticket_deps` is an MCP-layer function inside `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and is not importable as a library call. An implementor following the plan as written would reference non-existent symbols.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Supersede mechanism section, replace: 'read it via `rebar.show`/`ticket_deps`' with: 'read it via `rebar.show_ticket(child_id, repo_root=repo_root)` (for `parent_id`) and `rebar.deps(child_id, repo_root=repo_root)` (for `deps[]` entries carrying `{target_id, relation}`)'. Both are importable from `src/rebar/__init__.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC clarifying how `duplicates` link relations are treated in `delivered_now` (e.g., explicitly excluded, or also trigger delivered-elsewhere under the same sibling+live-vehicle conditions), or remove the mention of `duplicates` from the What section if it is only an implementation-detail of the query API.
findings.finding: The 'What' section mentions reading both `supersedes` AND `duplicates` links via `src/rebar/__init__.py`, but no AC addresses whether `duplicates` link relations also trigger the delivered-elsewhere path or are explicitly excluded — leaving an ambiguous, uncovered code path.
findings.suggested_fix: If `duplicates` links are only mentioned because the `ticket_deps` API returns them alongside `supersedes` and they are filtered out, note that explicitly in the What section (e.g., 'the implementation queries both supersedes and duplicates edges but only supersedes triggers the delivered-elsewhere path'). If duplicates can also signal delivered-elsewhere, add a corresponding AC and unit-test case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit test cases for: (a) parent epic reopen leaves closed child as delivered-now, (b) child's own reopen flips delivered_now to False, and (c) abandoned child returns False — and list these in the AC's unit-test enumeration.
findings.finding: The 'Acceptance Criteria (reopen semantics)' section introduces a new behavioral requirement — parent reopen does NOT un-deliver closed children, but a child's own reopen DOES flip it to not-delivered — yet the unit test list under the main AC names no test case for either direction of reopen semantics. Additionally, the `abandoned` case is described in the 'What' section as returning False but is absent from the named test cases.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (reopen semantics) section; main AC unit-test list: 'Unit tests cover attested / force-closed / drift-stale / supersede-sibling / supersede-nonsibling / in-progress cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC unit-test list to: '… / in-progress / abandoned / parent-reopened-child-still-delivered / child-reopened-not-delivered cases' and add corresponding test functions in the test file for `delivered_now`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove 'duplicates' from the 'What' section (if only 'supersedes' triggers delivered_now), or add an explicit AC defining whether and how a 'duplicates' link relation qualifies a child as delivered-elsewhere.
findings.finding: The 'What' section says to read 'supersedes/duplicates' links, but no AC defines whether a 'duplicates' link relation triggers delivered_now. The Mechanism section and all ACs refer only to 'supersedes'. It is unspecified whether 'A duplicates B' (or 'B duplicated_by A') should also count as delivered-elsewhere, and if so, under what conditions.
findings.location: What section: 'read the supersedes/duplicates links via src/rebar/__init__.py'; AC section: all ACs reference only 'supersede-sibling' / 'supersede-nonsibling'
findings.suggested_fix: If duplicates should not trigger delivered_now, remove '/duplicates' from the What section. If it should, add an AC: '- [ ] A `duplicates` link (A duplicates B, same parent) does / does not count as delivered-elsewhere, with a test asserting the choice.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete schema mapping for colloquial terms used in ACs: specify that 'force-closed' means `status=closed AND compute_validity(..., 'completion-verifier').valid == False` (no valid attestation), and 'abandoned' means `status` in {`archived`, `deleted`} OR `status=closed` without attestation — so implementers and test authors share an unambiguous decision rule.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'force-closed' and 'abandoned' as status-like terms in ACs and prose ('False for force-closed ... and abandoned children'; 'A is not itself force-closed/abandoned'), but the ticket_status schema enum is {open, in_progress, blocked, closed, archived, deleted} — neither 'force-closed' nor 'abandoned' is a real status value. The predicate's decision rule for mapping these colloquial terms to actual schema states (e.g., closed-without-signature = force-closed; deleted/archived = abandoned?) is never defined.
findings.location: AC: 'False for force-closed, unsigned, drift-stale, in-progress, and abandoned children'; AC (supersede): 'False when A is not a sibling or is itself force-closed/abandoned'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/test_delivered_now.py -v`) for each AC block so that an executor can produce verifiable evidence of passage.
findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any acceptance criterion — the plan says 'unit tests cover X cases' but never states the command (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/llm/plan_review/test_attest.py`) that would produce verifiable evidence of passage.
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Verification' section: `pytest tests/unit/test_delivered_now.py -v` (or the canonical test path) with expected output (all named cases pass). Reference the test file explicitly so CI and a human reviewer both know which command to run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the `delivered_now` contract: validity-on-read semantics, supersede-sibling conditions, and reopen-keyed-to-child rule — so Pass-3/Pass-2 implementers have a stable reference independent of this story's prose.
findings.finding: The `delivered_now` predicate establishes a new cross-component contract (attest.py → completion.py's compute_validity + rebar's link-relation API) that Pass-3 wiring and Pass-2 sub-calls will depend on, but no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is planned to capture the decision: what constitutes 'delivered' (validity-on-read, supersede-sibling semantics, reopen semantics). Future implementers of Pass-3/Pass-2 have no canonical reference beyond this story's prose.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Why / ## What — no doc/ADR update mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-delivered-now-predicate.md` (or append to an existing delivery-semantics ADR) summarising: (1) closed+valid-attestation path, (2) supersede-sibling path with liveness constraint, (3) reopen keys on child's own last_reopened_at, (4) no persisted state. Reference it from the Scope section of this story and from CLAUDE.md under 'Epic review pipeline'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that at least one concrete call-site (Pass-3 wiring or Pass-2 sub-call) is included in this story or the immediately following story, OR document in the plan why building `delivered_now` as a standalone precondition story is the intended vertical-slice pattern (mitigates premature-abstraction risk).
findings.finding: `delivered_now` will have zero production call-sites at the time this story is delivered, because both consumers (Pass-3 wiring and Pass-2 sub-call) are explicitly scoped OUT to separate stories. By the Rule-of-Three, a new abstraction with no existing call-sites is premature until at least one consumer lands.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'OUT: the Pass-3 wiring (separate story); the Pass-2 sub-call (separate story).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
